A system is deployed under a fixed memory budget, but “memory” cannot be treated as a single scalar constraint. If compute, latency, energy, and auditability matter as much as parameter count, then any retained representation must be judged not only by how much storage it uses, but by how expensive it is to access, update, explain, and verify. A compact model that requires costly recomputation or opaque compression may violate the budget in practice even if its parameter count is small. So the budget is multidimensional and unstable under operational demands.

Into that system, data arrive over time. If one describes the problem as class-incremental, one may think the main challenge is adding new labels while preserving old ones. But the premises say this obscures a deeper issue: concept-incremental change, where the features themselves shift meaning over time. Then stored feature representations are not neutral assets. A feature retained for efficiency or audit may later encode something different from what it originally encoded. What was once evidence for one concept may become misleading evidence for another. This means memory is not just limited; it can become semantically stale.

The difficulty increases further when the environment changes in ways that invalidate the original task definitions rather than merely adding new classes. In that case, forgetting is not simply a failure to preserve prior performance. Some prior competencies are tied to tasks that no longer exist in their original form. Auditability becomes harder because the question “why did the model make this decision?” presupposes a stable task ontology. If the ontology itself has shifted, then old audits and benchmarks no longer cleanly apply. A system may be simultaneously faithful to its stored past and inappropriate for the present.

The last two premises introduce objects or propositions—“Goschen uredidia semple intergraft the subtarsal hush” and “Monocyclica nonextinguished headshrinker wites huastec”—that carry no established interpretation in the surrounding framework. Taken as true, they still have consequences. First, the world the system faces contains truths that may not be representable within its existing feature space or task definitions. Second, auditability pressure does not disappear in the presence of such truths; the system must either allocate memory and explanatory machinery for uninterpreted but asserted structure, or leave parts of reality unassimilated. Either choice consumes resources in the multidimensional budget.

So the collision of premises implies a continual learner cannot rely on a static notion of task, a static notion of feature, or a static notion of memory. The harder the environment shifts, the less meaningful parameter preservation alone becomes. And if true statements can appear that do not fit the established conceptual scheme at all, then continual learning is not merely about retaining and extending knowledge, but about surviving repeated failures of representation under constrained, auditable, energy- and latency-bounded memory.
